meta.title: DBX-DEA-2: ELT Pipelines and Incremental Processing

domains.name: ELT with Spark SQL and Python

questions.stem: A company currently stores all its data in an Amazon S3 data lake with no transactional guarantees. They want to add ACID transactions and enable direct BI tool queries without moving the data. Which technology should they adopt?

options.text: Add Delta Lake on top of their existing S3 storage to enable ACID transactions while keeping data in open formats
Replace S3 with a relational data warehouse that supports ACID and SQL
Convert all Parquet files to CSV format, which natively supports ACID transactions
Create a separate OLTP database and synchronize it with S3 every night

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Delta Lake can be layered on top of existing object storage (like S3) to provide ACID transactions, schema enforcement, and time travel without requiring data migration to a new system. It stores data as Parquet files plus a transaction log, enabling BI tools to query directly via Delta-compatible connectors.

**B** is incorrect because replacing S3 with a relational warehouse requires moving data and switching to a proprietary format.

**C** is incorrect because CSV has no ACID support — it is a plain text format with no transactional guarantees.

**D** is incorrect because a nightly sync to a separate OLTP database introduces hours of data latency and consistency challenges when the two systems diverge.

[What is Delta Lake in Databricks?](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/delta-intro.html)

questions.stem: A Databricks job fails intermittently due to spot instance preemptions. The engineer wants to reduce cost while minimizing the impact of preemptions. Which cluster configuration BEST addresses this?

options.text: Use only on-demand worker instances with autoscaling disabled to guarantee availability
Use spot instances for both the driver and all workers to maximize cost savings
Use a mix of on-demand instances for the driver and spot instances for workers, with a fallback to on-demand for workers if spot is unavailable
Disable autoscaling and set a fixed worker count of 1 to reduce the surface area for preemptions

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. A common best practice is to use a reliable on-demand instance for the driver (to avoid losing job state if the driver is preempted) and spot instances for workers (where preemption causes a task retry, not a full job failure). This balances cost savings with reliability.

**A** is incorrect because using only on-demand instances for all workers maximizes cost without taking advantage of spot savings.

**B** is incorrect because using spot instances for both driver and workers is the highest-risk configuration — a preempted driver terminates the entire job.

**D** is incorrect because a single fixed worker provides minimal parallelism, poor fault tolerance, and does not address the preemption problem.

[Compute configuration reference](https://docs.databricks.com/compute/configure.html)

questions.stem:
A notebook contains the following cell:

```
%md
## Data Pipeline Documentation
This notebook ingests raw sales data from the landing zone.
```

What does this cell render?

options.text: It renders the text as formatted Markdown in the notebook output, displaying a heading and paragraph
It runs the string as a shell command on the cluster driver node
It executes the text as a Python multi-line string, printing it to the console
It creates a new notebook titled 'Data Pipeline Documentation'

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. The `%md` magic command tells Databricks to render the cell content as Markdown. `##` creates a second-level heading and the following text appears as a paragraph. This is used for documentation within notebooks.

**B** is incorrect because shell commands use the `%sh` magic command, not `%md`.

**C** is incorrect because Python multi-line strings use the default Python mode or `%python` — `%md` never executes Python code.

**D** is incorrect because `%md` has no notebook creation functionality; it only renders formatted text in the cell output.

[Develop code in Databricks notebooks](https://docs.databricks.com/notebooks/notebooks-use.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer is explaining Delta Lake's schema enforcement to a colleague. Select TWO statements that correctly describe schema enforcement in Delta Lake. (Select TWO)

options.text: A write that includes columns not present in the table schema will be rejected by default
Schema enforcement prevents writing data with wrong data types to an existing Delta table
Schema enforcement is disabled by default and must be explicitly enabled with a table property
Schema enforcement allows any new column to be automatically added to the table during a write

questions.explanation:
**A** and **B** are correct. Delta Lake enforces schema on write by default: if a DataFrame has columns that don't exist in the table schema (**A**), or if column data types don't match (**B**), the write is rejected and an error is thrown.

**C** is incorrect because schema enforcement is ON by default — no explicit configuration is needed to enable it.

**D** is incorrect because automatically adding new columns is schema evolution (enabled with the `mergeSchema` write option), not schema enforcement — by default, extra columns cause a write failure.

[What is Delta Lake in Databricks?](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/delta-intro.html)

questions.stem: Which statement correctly describes the DBFS (Databricks File System)?

options.text: DBFS is an abstraction layer over object storage (such as S3 or ADLS) that allows files to be accessed using file-system-like paths
DBFS is a dedicated block storage system that stores data on the cluster's local SSD drives only
DBFS stores data in a relational format that is incompatible with Delta Lake tables
DBFS is equivalent to Unity Catalog and replaces it in Databricks workspaces

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. DBFS is a distributed file system layer built into Databricks that maps file-system-like paths (e.g., `dbfs:/data/`) to underlying cloud object storage such as AWS S3, Azure ADLS, or Google Cloud Storage. This allows code to use familiar path-based APIs without knowing the cloud storage details.

**B** is incorrect because DBFS is not local SSD storage — it is backed by cloud object storage, not the cluster's local drives.

**C** is incorrect because DBFS is fully compatible with Delta Lake, which stores its Parquet files and transaction log in object storage (accessible via DBFS).

**D** is incorrect because DBFS and Unity Catalog serve completely different purposes; Unity Catalog is a data governance layer, not a file storage abstraction.

[What is DBFS?](https://docs.databricks.com/dbfs/index.html)

questions.stem:
A data engineer queries a Delta table using time travel:

```sql
SELECT * FROM orders VERSION AS OF 5;
```

The query fails with an error stating the version is not available. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?

options.text: The table has fewer than 5 total versions in its history
Time travel using `VERSION AS OF` is only supported in Python, not in SQL
The Delta table must be converted to a view before time travel queries are supported
`VACUUM` was previously run, removing the data files corresponding to version 5, and the default retention period (7 days) has passed

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. `VACUUM` removes Parquet data files that are no longer referenced by the current table version and are older than the retention threshold (default 7 days). If `VACUUM` has deleted the files that version 5 pointed to, time travel to that version fails because the underlying data files no longer exist.

**A** is incorrect because having fewer than 5 total versions would produce a different error message (version not found in log) — the version entry still exists in the transaction log but its data files are gone after VACUUM.

**B** is incorrect because `VERSION AS OF` is valid Spark SQL syntax and works in SQL queries.

**C** is incorrect because time travel works on Delta tables directly — no conversion to a view is required or supported for this purpose.

[VACUUM](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/delta-vacuum.html)

questions.stem: A cluster policy is applied to a team's Databricks workspace. A junior engineer tries to create an all-purpose cluster with 20 worker nodes but receives a permission error. What is the MOST LIKELY reason?

options.text: The cluster policy restricts the maximum number of worker nodes, and the engineer's request exceeds that limit
Only Databricks admins can create clusters with more than 10 workers regardless of any policy settings
The engineer is using a job cluster configuration instead of an all-purpose cluster configuration
The worker count setting is locked globally across all Databricks accounts to a maximum of 10

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Cluster policies allow administrators to define rules that restrict cluster configuration options — including maximum worker count, allowed instance types, runtime versions, and more. If a policy limits the maximum workers to fewer than 20, any attempt to create a cluster exceeding that limit will fail with a policy violation error.

**B** is incorrect because cluster size limits are set by policies, not by a hard-coded global Databricks limit tied to admin status.

**C** is incorrect because the issue described relates to policy enforcement, not the type of cluster being created.

**D** is incorrect because there is no universal 10-worker cap in Databricks — limits are set per workspace through cluster policies.

[Compute configuration reference](https://docs.databricks.com/compute/configure.html)

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates the following object:

```sql
CREATE GLOBAL TEMP VIEW weekly_summary
AS SELECT week, SUM(sales) FROM transactions GROUP BY week;
```

How is this view accessed from another notebook in the same cluster?

options.text: `SELECT * FROM weekly_summary`
`SELECT * FROM global_temp.weekly_summary`
`SELECT * FROM temp.weekly_summary`
`SELECT * FROM default.weekly_summary`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Global temporary views are stored in the special `global_temp` database in Databricks. To query a global temp view from any session on the same cluster, you must use the fully qualified name `global_temp.<view_name>`.

**A** is incorrect because without a qualifier, Spark would look for `weekly_summary` in the current session's scope or the default schema, not in `global_temp`, and the query would fail.

**C** is incorrect because `temp` is not a valid database name for global temp views — only `global_temp` is the correct qualifier.

**D** is incorrect because `default` is the standard default schema for persistent tables, not where global temp views are stored.

[CREATE VIEW](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-syntax-ddl-create-view.html)

questions.stem:
A data engineer writes a Python UDF in Databricks:

```python
from pyspark.sql.functions import udf
from pyspark.sql.types import StringType

@udf(returnType=StringType())
def mask_email(email):
    if email is None:
        return None
    parts = email.split('@')
    return '***@' + parts[1] if len(parts) == 2 else email

df = df.withColumn('masked_email', mask_email('email'))
```

What must be done to use this UDF in Spark SQL queries instead of only in DataFrame API calls?

options.text: Nothing — Python UDFs decorated with `@udf` are automatically available in Spark SQL
Convert the UDF to a Pandas UDF before it can be used in SQL
Register the UDF with `spark.udf.register('mask_email', mask_email)` so it can be called by name in SQL
Rewrite the UDF using `CREATE FUNCTION` in SQL; Python UDFs cannot be used in SQL queries

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Python UDFs created with the `@udf` decorator are only usable in the DataFrame API by default. To call them in Spark SQL (e.g., `SELECT mask_email(email) FROM ...`), you must register them with `spark.udf.register('function_name', function)`, which makes them available by name in SQL contexts.

**A** is incorrect because the `@udf` decorator does not automatically register the function in the SQL namespace — explicit registration is always required.

**B** is incorrect because Pandas UDFs have the same SQL registration requirement; converting to a Pandas UDF does not automatically expose it in SQL.

**D** is incorrect because Python UDFs can be used in SQL after registration via `spark.udf.register` — rewriting in SQL is unnecessary.

[User-defined scalar functions - Python](https://docs.databricks.com/udf/python.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to write a DataFrame to a Delta table, replacing all existing data if the table already exists. Which write mode should they use?

options.text: `df.write.format('delta').mode('append').save('/tables/orders')`
`df.write.format('delta').mode('merge').save('/tables/orders')`
`df.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').save('/tables/orders')`
`df.write.format('delta').mode('update').save('/tables/orders')`

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. `mode('overwrite')` replaces all existing data in the target location with the new DataFrame contents. It is the correct write mode when you want a full replacement of the table data.

**A** is incorrect because `mode('append')` adds new rows to the existing table without removing old data — the existing records remain.

**B** is incorrect because `'merge'` is not a valid write mode in the DataFrame writer API — `MERGE INTO` is a separate SQL DML command and cannot be specified as a `.mode()` argument.

**D** is incorrect because `'update'` is not a valid write mode in Spark's DataFrame writer API.

[MERGE INTO](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/delta-merge-into.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer has a table with a column `order_items` of type `ARRAY<STRUCT<product: STRING, qty: INT>>`. They want to create one row per item. Which function should they use?

options.text: `FILTER(order_items, item -> item.qty > 0)`
`TRANSFORM(order_items, item -> item.product)`
`EXPLODE(order_items)`
`FLATTEN(order_items)`

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. `EXPLODE` takes an array or map column and produces one output row for each element in the array, effectively unpacking the nested structure into multiple rows. Each element of `order_items` (a struct) becomes its own row with the struct's fields accessible as columns.

**A** is incorrect because `FILTER` keeps or removes elements within the array based on a condition, but the result is still a single array per original row — it does not create additional rows.

**B** is incorrect because `TRANSFORM` applies a function to each element and returns a new array of the same length — still one row per original row, just with transformed array contents.

**D** is incorrect because `FLATTEN` converts an array of arrays into a single flat array — it does not apply to an array of structs and does not create new rows.

[explode table-valued generator function](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/sql/language-manual/functions/explode)

questions.stem:
A data engineer needs to categorize orders by size:
- 'large' if `total > 1000`
- 'medium' if `total > 100`
- 'small' otherwise

Which SQL expression correctly implements this?

options.text: `IF(total > 1000, 'large', IF(total > 100, 'medium', 'small'))`
`CASE WHEN total > 1000 THEN 'large' WHEN total > 100 THEN 'medium' ELSE 'small' END`
`COALESCE(total > 1000, 'large', total > 100, 'medium', 'small')`
`NVL(total, 'large')`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. The `CASE WHEN ... THEN ... ELSE ... END` expression is the standard SQL way to evaluate multiple conditions sequentially and return the first matching result. Spark SQL evaluates `WHEN` clauses in order, so a `total` of 1500 matches the first clause ('large'); a `total` of 500 falls through to the second ('medium'); anything else returns 'small'.

**A** is a technically valid alternative using nested `IF` calls, but `CASE WHEN` is the idiomatic, readable SQL form and the canonical exam answer for multi-branch conditional logic.

**C** is incorrect because `COALESCE` returns the first non-NULL value from a list of expressions — it cannot evaluate conditional expressions that yield categorized strings.

**D** is incorrect because `NVL(total, 'large')` replaces NULL values with a default — it has no conditional categorization capability.

[CASE expression](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/functions/case.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer is choosing between Parquet and Delta formats for a production pipeline. Select TWO advantages that Delta format has over plain Parquet. (Select TWO)

options.text: Delta supports ACID transactions, enabling reliable concurrent reads and writes
Delta files are smaller than Parquet files because Delta uses a proprietary compression algorithm
Delta supports `DELETE`, `UPDATE`, and `MERGE INTO` operations without full table rewrites via the transaction log
Delta tables can only be read by Databricks, whereas Parquet can be read by any Spark-compatible tool

questions.explanation:
**A** and **C** are correct. Delta Lake adds ACID transaction guarantees over Parquet files (**A**), and it enables row-level `DELETE`, `UPDATE`, and `MERGE` operations that standard Parquet does not support — Delta manages this through its transaction log and by rewriting only affected files (**C**).

**B** is incorrect because Delta stores data as Parquet files underneath — the compression format is the same; Delta does not introduce a proprietary compression algorithm.

**D** is incorrect because Delta Lake is open source and its format can be read by many tools beyond Databricks, including Apache Spark, Flink, Presto, and others via the open Delta protocol.

[What is Delta Lake in Databricks?](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/delta-intro.html)

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates a SQL UDF in Databricks:

```sql
CREATE FUNCTION calculate_tax(price DOUBLE, rate DOUBLE)
RETURNS DOUBLE
RETURN price * rate;
```

Which statement about this SQL UDF is correct?

options.text: This UDF can be called in `WHERE`, `SELECT`, and `HAVING` clauses in Spark SQL queries
SQL UDFs cannot be used inside `SELECT` statements; they are only for `WHERE` clause filtering
This UDF is session-scoped and is dropped when the notebook session ends
SQL UDFs defined with `CREATE FUNCTION` cannot accept more than one argument

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. A SQL UDF created with `CREATE FUNCTION` is persisted in the metastore (unlike Python UDFs, which are session-scoped) and can be called anywhere a function call is valid in Spark SQL — including `SELECT`, `WHERE`, `HAVING`, `ORDER BY`, and other clauses.

**B** is incorrect because SQL UDFs are most commonly used in `SELECT` clauses to transform or compute column values — restricting them to `WHERE` clauses only would make them nearly useless.

**C** is incorrect because SQL UDFs are persisted in the metastore (not session-scoped); it is Python UDFs registered with `spark.udf.register` that are session-scoped.

**D** is incorrect because `CREATE FUNCTION` fully supports multiple arguments, as demonstrated by the two-parameter `calculate_tax` example in the stem.

[User-defined functions](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/udf/)

questions.stem:
A data engineer reads a JSON file where each line is a separate JSON object (JSON Lines format):

```python
df = spark.read.format('json').load('/data/events.jsonl')
```

The resulting schema shows all columns as `STRING` type even though some fields contain integers. What option should be added to infer the correct types?

options.text: `.option('inferSchema', 'true')`
`.option('multiLine', 'true')`
`.option('header', 'true')`
`.option('escape', '"')`

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. By default, Spark reads JSON with `inferSchema` disabled, treating all values as strings. Adding `.option('inferSchema', 'true')` causes Spark to sample the data and infer more specific types like `INT`, `DOUBLE`, and `BOOLEAN`.

**B** is incorrect because `multiLine` is used when a single JSON object spans multiple lines — in JSON Lines format (the default), each line is already a complete JSON object, so this option is irrelevant to type inference.

**C** is incorrect because `header` is a CSV-specific option for treating the first row as column names — it does not apply to JSON files at all.

**D** is incorrect because `escape` controls the escape character handling within JSON strings, which has no effect on data type inference.

[What is Delta Lake in Databricks?](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/delta-intro.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst asks a data engineer what the difference is between a CTE and a temporary view. Which answer is MOST accurate?

options.text: A CTE is scoped to a single query statement; a temp view persists for the entire Spark session and can be referenced in multiple subsequent queries
A CTE persists in the metastore; a temp view is dropped at the end of the current cell
A CTE can only be used once; a temp view can be referenced unlimited times
There is no functional difference; CTEs and temp views are interchangeable in all contexts

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. A CTE (`WITH ... AS (...)`) exists only within the single SQL statement that defines it and cannot be referenced by a later statement. A `CREATE TEMP VIEW` persists for the lifetime of the Spark session and can be queried by any subsequent statement in that session.

**B** is incorrect because CTEs are not persisted in the metastore — it is permanent (non-temporary) views that are persisted there.

**C** is incorrect because a CTE can be referenced multiple times within the same query — the limitation is that it cannot be referenced across multiple separate SQL statements.

**D** is incorrect because CTEs and temp views have meaningfully different scopes and lifetimes: CTEs are query-scoped, temp views are session-scoped.

[CREATE VIEW](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-syntax-ddl-create-view.html)

questions.stem: An Auto Loader stream is configured with a `checkpointLocation`. The stream is stopped and restarted several hours later. What happens when the stream restarts?

options.text: The stream reprocesses all files from the beginning of the source directory
The stream resumes from where it stopped, processing only files that arrived after the checkpoint was last written
The stream requires a manual reset command before it can continue reading new files
The stream deletes all previously processed files from the source directory before resuming

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. The `checkpointLocation` stores the state of the stream — specifically which files have been processed and the stream's progress offset. When the stream restarts, it reads the checkpoint and resumes exactly from where it left off, processing only new files that arrived after the last checkpoint.

**A** is incorrect because reprocessing all files from the beginning is the behavior when no checkpoint exists or when the checkpoint is deleted before restarting.

**C** is incorrect because no manual reset command is needed — the checkpoint is read automatically on stream startup.

**D** is incorrect because Structured Streaming never deletes source data; the checkpoint only tracks which files have been read, not the source files themselves.

[What is Auto Loader?](https://docs.databricks.com/ingestion/auto-loader/index.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer compares `COPY INTO` and Auto Loader for loading files from cloud storage into Delta tables. Which statement CORRECTLY differentiates the two?

options.text: `COPY INTO` is a streaming operation; Auto Loader is a batch operation
Both `COPY INTO` and Auto Loader produce identical results in all scenarios and are interchangeable
`COPY INTO` is best for loading a fixed set of files idempotently in a batch; Auto Loader is better for continuously ingesting files as they arrive using a streaming approach
`COPY INTO` supports schema evolution; Auto Loader does not

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. `COPY INTO` is a SQL command designed for idempotent batch loading of a specific set of files — it tracks loaded files and skips them on re-runs, making it ideal for triggered batch loads. Auto Loader is a Structured Streaming source (`cloudFiles`) designed for continuous, near-real-time ingestion as new files arrive.

**A** is incorrect because the descriptions are reversed — `COPY INTO` is the batch operation and Auto Loader is the streaming operation, not the other way around.

**B** is incorrect because `COPY INTO` and Auto Loader have different operational models, use cases, and scalability characteristics — they are not interchangeable.

**D** is incorrect because Auto Loader supports schema evolution via `cloudFiles.schemaHints` and rescue columns, while `COPY INTO` has more limited schema handling — not the reverse.

[What is Auto Loader?](https://docs.databricks.com/ingestion/auto-loader/index.html)

questions.stem: A streaming job reads from a Delta table and appends results to another Delta table. The engineer notices that every time the cluster is restarted, the stream reprocesses some data already in the output table, creating duplicate rows. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?

options.text: The `checkpointLocation` is set to a path that is deleted or not persisted between cluster restarts
Delta table sinks do not support streaming writes and require special configuration
The `outputMode('append')` does not work with Delta sinks — `outputMode('complete')` must be used
The source Delta table does not have the transaction log enabled

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. The checkpoint location stores the stream's progress (which offsets or Delta log versions have been processed). If the checkpoint is in a location that is ephemeral (e.g., local disk on the driver node) or is deleted between restarts, the stream cannot determine where it stopped and reprocesses data from an earlier point, causing duplicates. The fix is to store the checkpoint in durable storage such as a cloud storage path.

**B** is incorrect because Delta tables are first-class streaming sinks in Structured Streaming and require no special configuration beyond a checkpoint location.

**C** is incorrect because `outputMode('append')` is the correct and commonly used mode for Delta sinks receiving streaming inserts.

**D** is incorrect because Delta tables always have a transaction log — this cannot be disabled, and its absence is not a valid error condition.

[Delta Lake table streaming reads and writes](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/delta-streaming.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer is designing an incremental pipeline that reads from a Delta source table. Select TWO statements that are true about reading a Delta table as a Structured Stream. (Select TWO)

options.text: The stream reads new rows added to the Delta table (inserts) as incremental data in each micro-batch
The stream automatically processes `DELETE` and `UPDATE` operations from the source Delta table as change data
Using `option('maxFilesPerTrigger', '1')` limits each micro-batch to processing changes from at most 1 Delta commit at a time
Reading a Delta table as a stream requires Unity Catalog and cannot be done with DBFS-backed tables

questions.explanation:
**A** and **C** are correct. When a Delta table is read as a stream using `readStream`, each micro-batch processes new inserts (files added by new commits) since the last checkpoint position — this is the default append-only stream behavior (**A**). The `maxFilesPerTrigger` option (**C**) controls the rate of processing by limiting how many new files from Delta commits are included in each micro-batch.

**B** is incorrect because standard Delta streaming reads do not automatically expose `UPDATE` and `DELETE` changes as events — that requires enabling the Delta Change Data Feed feature and reading with `readChangeFeed`.

**D** is incorrect because streaming from Delta tables works with DBFS-backed tables and does not require Unity Catalog.

[Use change data feed on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/delta-change-data-feed.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer has a streaming aggregation that counts events per user per 5-minute window. They want to use `outputMode('append')` to write only finalized window results to the sink. What is REQUIRED to make this work correctly?

options.text: Set `trigger(processingTime='5 minutes')` to match the window size
Add `withWatermark('event_time', '10 minutes')` to define the late data threshold so Spark knows when windows are finalized
Use `outputMode('complete')` instead — `outputMode('append')` cannot be combined with window aggregations
Add `repartition(1)` before the aggregation to ensure all events for a window are on the same partition

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. For append mode to work with streaming windowed aggregations, Spark needs to know when a window is definitively closed so it can emit the final result. A watermark defines how long Spark waits for late data. Once the watermark passes the end of a window, that window is finalized and its result can be emitted in append mode. Without a watermark, Spark cannot determine finalization and will raise an error if append mode is requested.

**A** is incorrect because the trigger interval controls how often micro-batches run but does not determine when windows are finalized — a 5-minute trigger interval and a 5-minute window are independent concepts.

**C** is incorrect because append mode combined with watermarking is a valid and recommended pattern for windowed aggregations; complete mode re-emits the entire result set every batch.

**D** is incorrect because the number of partitions does not affect output mode requirements — this is an optimization concern, not a correctness concern.

[Configure Structured Streaming trigger intervals](https://docs.databricks.com/structured-streaming/triggers.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `COPY INTO` to load JSON files from `s3://bucket/events/2024/` into a Delta table. Two weeks later, they run the same command again. New files have been added to the path. What is the result of the second run?

options.text: The second run loads only the new files added since the first run, appending them to the Delta table
The second run fails because `COPY INTO` can only be run once per target table
The second run reloads all files (original and new), resulting in duplicate data
The second run overwrites the Delta table with only the new files

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. `COPY INTO` tracks which files have been loaded into the target Delta table (stored in the Delta transaction log). On subsequent runs, it automatically skips files that were loaded in previous runs and processes only the newly added files. This idempotent incremental loading is one of the key advantages of `COPY INTO`.

**B** is incorrect because `COPY INTO` is explicitly designed to be run multiple times — idempotency is a core feature, not a limitation.

**C** is incorrect because the file-tracking mechanism prevents duplicate loading of files that were already processed in earlier runs.

**D** is incorrect because `COPY INTO` appends new data to the existing table; it does not overwrite or truncate previous data.

[What is Auto Loader?](https://docs.databricks.com/ingestion/auto-loader/index.html)

questions.stem: A data engineering team wants to define a Delta Live Tables pipeline. Which decorator is used to define a DLT table in Python?

options.text: `@spark.table`
`@delta.table`
`@pipeline.create_table`
`@dlt.table`

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. In Delta Live Tables, the `@dlt.table` decorator (from the `dlt` module) is used to define a table in a DLT pipeline. The decorated function returns a DataFrame that defines the table's contents, and DLT handles materialization, dependency tracking, and lineage automatically.

**A** is incorrect because `@spark.table` is not a valid decorator in any Databricks or PySpark API.

**B** is incorrect because `@delta.table` is not a valid decorator — the correct module name for DLT is `dlt`, not `delta`.

**C** is incorrect because `@pipeline.create_table` does not exist as a DLT construct — DLT uses the `dlt` module, not a `pipeline` module.

[Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines](https://docs.databricks.com/delta-live-tables/index.html)

questions.stem: A Databricks Job task is configured with a retry policy of 2 retries and a retry interval of 5 minutes. The task fails on the first attempt. How many total attempts will be made before the task is marked as permanently failed?

options.text: 1 attempt (the original only — retries require manual triggering)
2 attempts (the original plus 1 retry)
3 attempts (the original plus 2 retries)
4 attempts (the original plus 3 automatic retries)

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. A retry count of 2 means Databricks will retry the task up to 2 times after the initial failure. The total number of attempts is: 1 original attempt + 2 retries = 3 total attempts. Only after all retry attempts are exhausted is the task marked as permanently failed.

**A** is incorrect because retries are automatic based on the configured policy — no manual triggering is required.

**B** is incorrect because 2 retries means 2 additional attempts after the first failure, totaling 3 attempts, not 2.

**D** is incorrect because a retry count of 2 gives exactly 3 total attempts (original + 2 retries), not 4.

[Lakeflow Jobs](https://docs.databricks.com/jobs/index.html)

questions.stem: A DLT pipeline is in development mode. How does development mode differ from production mode?

options.text: Development mode runs the pipeline on a job cluster that is terminated after each run; production mode keeps the cluster running
Development mode disables all DLT expectations so data quality checks are skipped during testing
Development mode keeps the cluster running between updates for faster iteration and does not retry on failure; production mode terminates the cluster after each run and retries on failure
Development mode writes output tables to a temporary schema that is dropped after the pipeline run

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. In DLT development mode, the pipeline cluster is kept alive between runs to avoid cluster startup overhead during iterative development, and failures are not automatically retried so engineers can see errors quickly. In production mode, the cluster is terminated after each run to minimize cost, and automatic retries are enabled for reliability.

**A** is incorrect because the cluster behavior described is reversed — in development mode the cluster stays alive; in production mode it is terminated after each run.

**B** is incorrect because DLT expectations (data quality constraints) run in both development and production modes — development mode does not skip data quality checks.

**D** is incorrect because development mode still writes to the configured output schema; the outputs are not discarded or written to a temporary location.

[Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines](https://docs.databricks.com/delta-live-tables/index.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to be notified when a Databricks Job fails. Select TWO ways to configure failure notifications in Databricks Jobs. (Select TWO)

options.text: Configure an email alert in the Job's notification settings for the 'on failure' event
Add a `try/except` block in the notebook that sends an email using Python's `smtplib`
Configure a webhook notification in the Job's notification settings that posts to a webhook URL on failure
Set the `spark.databricks.notification.email` configuration property on the cluster

questions.explanation:
**A** and **C** are correct. Databricks Jobs natively supports two types of failure notifications configured in the Job settings: email alerts (**A**) sent to specified addresses on job/task events, and webhook notifications (**C**) that POST a JSON payload to a specified URL (e.g., a Slack webhook or PagerDuty endpoint).

**B** is incorrect because while sending email via `smtplib` is technically possible code, it is an anti-pattern: it requires the notebook to succeed far enough to reach the try/except block, and it bypasses the built-in notification system entirely.

**D** is incorrect because there is no `spark.databricks.notification.email` Spark configuration property — job notifications are configured at the job level in the Databricks UI or API, not as cluster properties.

[Lakeflow Jobs](https://docs.databricks.com/jobs/index.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `DESCRIBE HISTORY prod.sales.orders`. What information does this command return?

options.text: The current table schema including column names, data types, and nullable constraints
A log of all operations performed on the table, including the operation type, user, timestamp, and version number
The storage details of the table including file count, size, and storage location
The list of all users who currently have privileges on the table

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. `DESCRIBE HISTORY` reads the Delta transaction log and returns a history of all operations performed on the table, including the version number, timestamp, user who performed the operation, operation type (INSERT, MERGE, OPTIMIZE, etc.), and operation parameters. This command is used to audit changes and find version numbers for time travel.

**A** is incorrect because the current schema (column names, data types, nullable constraints) is returned by `DESCRIBE TABLE`, not `DESCRIBE HISTORY`.

**C** is incorrect because storage details such as file count, total size, and storage location are returned by `DESCRIBE DETAIL`, not `DESCRIBE HISTORY`.

**D** is incorrect because privilege listings require `SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE <name>`, not `DESCRIBE HISTORY`.

[VACUUM](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/delta-vacuum.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to add a description to a column in a Unity Catalog table. Which SQL command accomplishes this?

options.text: `ALTER TABLE orders ALTER COLUMN customer_id COMMENT 'Unique identifier for the customer'`
`UPDATE TABLE orders SET COMMENT ON customer_id = 'Unique identifier for the customer'`
`DESCRIBE TABLE orders SET COLUMN customer_id COMMENT 'Unique identifier for the customer'`
`INSERT INTO TABLE orders COMMENT 'Unique identifier for the customer' ON customer_id`

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. The `ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... COMMENT ...` syntax is the standard SQL command in Databricks/Spark SQL for adding or updating a column-level comment. This comment is stored in the metastore and appears when the table is described with `DESCRIBE TABLE`.

**B** is incorrect because `UPDATE TABLE ... SET COMMENT` is not valid Databricks SQL syntax — `UPDATE` is a DML command for modifying row data, not table metadata.

**C** is incorrect because `DESCRIBE TABLE` is a read command that displays table metadata — it cannot modify column comments.

**D** is incorrect because `INSERT INTO TABLE ... COMMENT` is not valid SQL syntax for any purpose in Databricks.

[What is Unity Catalog?](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/index.html)

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates a managed table in Unity Catalog:

```sql
CREATE TABLE prod.analytics.user_sessions
  (session_id STRING, user_id BIGINT, duration_sec INT);
```

A few weeks later, the table is dropped with `DROP TABLE prod.analytics.user_sessions`. What happens to the underlying data files?

options.text: The data files remain in their original storage location and must be manually deleted
The data files are moved to a Unity Catalog recycle bin and retained for 30 days
Unity Catalog deletes the underlying data files because this is a managed table
The data files are archived to a separate backup storage location in the metastore

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. For managed tables in Unity Catalog, Databricks controls both the metadata and the underlying data files. When a managed table is dropped, Unity Catalog deletes both the metadata entry and the underlying Parquet/Delta data files. This is the key distinction from external tables, where only the metadata is removed and data files are retained.

**A** is incorrect because leaving data files in place after a DROP is the behavior of external tables, not managed tables.

**B** is incorrect because Unity Catalog does not have a recycle bin for dropped managed table data — once dropped, the data is deleted without a built-in recovery mechanism.

**D** is incorrect because there is no automatic archival to backup storage when dropping a managed table in Unity Catalog.

[What is Unity Catalog?](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/index.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to grant a service principal the ability to query tables in the `prod.reporting` schema but NOT create or drop tables. What is the MINIMUM set of grants required in Unity Catalog?

options.text: `GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON SCHEMA prod.reporting TO service_principal`
`GRANT USAGE ON CATALOG prod TO service_principal; GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA prod.reporting TO service_principal; GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA prod.reporting TO service_principal`
`GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA prod.reporting TO service_principal`
`GRANT USAGE ON CATALOG prod TO service_principal; GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA prod.reporting TO service_principal`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Unity Catalog requires hierarchical USAGE grants to traverse the namespace, plus specific privileges on the objects. The minimum required is: `USAGE` on the catalog (to access `prod`), `USAGE` on the schema (to access `prod.reporting`), and `SELECT` on the tables (to read data). Without all three, the principal cannot reach the tables.

**A** is incorrect because `ALL PRIVILEGES` grants far more than needed — including `CREATE`, `MODIFY`, and ownership-level permissions — violating the principle of least privilege.

**C** is incorrect because `GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA` is not valid Unity Catalog syntax for granting table-level SELECT on all tables within a schema.

**D** is incorrect because it is missing `USAGE ON SCHEMA prod.reporting` — without schema USAGE, the principal cannot traverse into the schema even if catalog USAGE and table SELECT are granted.

[What is Unity Catalog?](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/index.html)

questions.stem: A data science team wants to run machine learning workloads in Databricks, including training a scikit-learn model and using MLflow for experiment tracking. Which Databricks Runtime should they use?

options.text: Standard Databricks Runtime (e.g., DBR 13.3 LTS), which includes all ML libraries pre-installed
Databricks Runtime for Genomics, which is optimized for scikit-learn workloads
Databricks Runtime for Machine Learning (e.g., DBR 13.3 LTS ML), which includes pre-installed ML libraries like scikit-learn, TensorFlow, PyTorch, and MLflow
Any runtime version — scikit-learn and MLflow must always be installed manually regardless of the runtime

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Databricks Runtime for Machine Learning (DBR ML) is a specialized runtime that comes pre-installed with popular ML frameworks including scikit-learn, TensorFlow, PyTorch, XGBoost, LightGBM, and MLflow. This eliminates the need to manually install these libraries for ML workloads.

**A** is incorrect because the standard DBR does not include scikit-learn, PyTorch, or TensorFlow by default — these must be manually installed as cluster libraries.

**B** is incorrect because the Databricks Runtime for Genomics is designed for bioinformatics workloads such as variant calling pipelines, not general ML workloads.

**D** is incorrect because the ML runtime specifically provides pre-installed ML libraries, avoiding manual installation — that is its primary purpose.

[What is a data lakehouse?](https://docs.databricks.com/lakehouse/index.html)

questions.stem: A platform engineer is evaluating Databricks Asset Bundles (DABs) for their team. Which statement BEST describes what Databricks Asset Bundles provide?

options.text: A Databricks Asset Bundle is a Delta table packaging format that bundles multiple tables into a single deployable artifact
Asset Bundles are a Databricks-specific archive format (.dab) for exporting notebooks that replaces the .dbc format
Databricks Asset Bundles provide a YAML-based configuration framework for defining, testing, and deploying Databricks resources (jobs, pipelines, notebooks) as code using the Databricks CLI
Asset Bundles are only available for deploying to Databricks on Azure and are not supported on AWS or GCP

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Databricks Asset Bundles (DABs) are a Databricks CLI feature that allows teams to define their Databricks resources — such as jobs, Delta Live Tables pipelines, notebooks, and clusters — as YAML configuration files. This enables Infrastructure-as-Code practices: resources can be version-controlled, templated, tested in staging environments, and deployed to production via CI/CD pipelines.

**A** is incorrect because DABs are not a Delta table format — they describe workspace resources (jobs, pipelines, notebooks) as code, not table packaging.

**B** is incorrect because DABs are not a notebook archive format; `.dbc` files are a separate, older concept for exporting and importing notebooks as a zip-like archive.

**D** is incorrect because Databricks Asset Bundles are available across all major cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) and are not limited to Azure.

[What is a data lakehouse?](https://docs.databricks.com/lakehouse/index.html)

questions.stem: An organization is migrating from a workspace-level Hive metastore to Unity Catalog. A data engineer compares the two approaches. Which statement CORRECTLY contrasts Unity Catalog with table ACLs on the Hive metastore?

options.text: Table ACLs on the Hive metastore provide catalog-level and cross-workspace governance; Unity Catalog does not
Both Table ACLs and Unity Catalog use identical GRANT syntax and provide the same level of governance

Table ACLs are workspace-scoped and do not support column-level security or row-level security natively; Unity Catalog provides governance across workspaces and includes column masking and row filtering
Unity Catalog replaces Parquet storage with a proprietary format to enforce row-level security

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Legacy table ACLs on the workspace Hive metastore are scoped to a single workspace, do not natively support column-level masking or row-level filtering, and have no cross-workspace sharing capability. Unity Catalog addresses these limitations with account-level governance spanning multiple workspaces, column masking, row filtering, and Delta Sharing.

**A** is incorrect because the descriptions are reversed — Unity Catalog provides cross-workspace governance, not legacy Table ACLs.

**B** is incorrect because while the GRANT/REVOKE syntax is similar, the governance capabilities are significantly different in scope and features.

**D** is incorrect because Unity Catalog uses the same open storage formats (Parquet/Delta) and does not replace them with a proprietary format to enforce security.

[What is Unity Catalog?](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/index.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to connect a Databricks cluster to an Azure Data Lake Storage (ADLS) account using the user's own Azure AD credentials rather than shared service principal credentials. Which feature enables this?

options.text: DBFS mount with a shared access signature (SAS) token stored in Databricks Secrets
A cluster init script that runs `az login` to authenticate each user on cluster startup
Credential passthrough, which forwards the user's Azure AD token to ADLS for authentication on access
Unity Catalog external locations with a storage credential based on a service principal

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Azure Active Directory (AAD) credential passthrough allows a Databricks cluster to forward the authenticated user's Azure AD credentials directly to ADLS Gen2 for access control. Each user can only access data they are authorized to access in ADLS based on their personal Azure AD permissions — no shared credentials are required.

**A** is incorrect because SAS tokens are shared secrets tied to storage account access, not user-specific Azure AD credentials.

**B** is incorrect because `az login` is an interactive CLI authentication command and is not a practical mechanism for cluster startup authentication — it cannot be reliably automated per-user in an init script.

**D** is incorrect because a Unity Catalog external location with a service principal uses a shared identity, not per-user credentials, which contradicts the requirement to forward individual user credentials.

[Azure Data Lake Storage credential passthrough](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/databricks/archive/credential-passthrough/adls-passthrough)

questions.stem: A data engineer has an array of key-value pairs in the form `ARRAY<STRUCT<key: STRING, value: STRING>>` named `attributes` and wants to convert it into a single `MAP<STRING, STRING>` column. Which function accomplishes this?

options.text: `EXPLODE(attributes)`
`map_from_entries(attributes)`
`FLATTEN(attributes)`
`TRANSFORM(attributes, a -> a.key)`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. `map_from_entries(array)` takes an array of structs where each struct has exactly two fields (key and value) and constructs a MAP from those entries. This is the purpose-built function for converting an array of key-value structs into a map.

**A** is incorrect because `EXPLODE` unpacks the array into multiple rows (one row per element), which is the opposite of consolidating into a map.

**C** is incorrect because `FLATTEN` merges an array of arrays into a single flat array — it does not create a map and does not apply to an array of structs.

**D** is incorrect because `TRANSFORM` applies a function to each element and returns a new array of the same size, not a map — the result would still be an array type.

[map_from_entries function](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/sql/language-manual/functions/map_from_entries)

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to find all customers in the `customers` table who do NOT have any orders in the `orders` table. Which query pattern returns the correct result?

options.text: `SELECT * FROM customers EXCEPT SELECT customer_id FROM orders`
`SELECT c.* FROM customers c WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id)`
`SELECT * FROM customers INNER JOIN orders USING (customer_id)`
`SELECT * FROM customers UNION SELECT * FROM orders`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. The `NOT EXISTS` subquery pattern returns all customers for whom no matching row exists in the `orders` table — exactly the semantics of 'customers without any orders'. The correlated subquery checks each customer's `customer_id` against the `orders` table individually.

**A** is incorrect because `EXCEPT` performs a set difference between two result sets with the same columns — selecting all columns from `customers` vs. only `customer_id` from `orders` produces a column count mismatch error, and the semantics would not match the requirement anyway.

**C** is incorrect because `INNER JOIN` returns only customers who DO have matching rows in `orders` — the exact opposite of the requirement.

**D** is incorrect because `UNION` combines rows from both tables into a single result set without filtering by existence — it does not identify customers who lack orders.

[WHERE clause and predicate subqueries](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-syntax-qry-select-where)

questions.stem: A data engineer writes a large DataFrame to a Delta table and wants to physically co-locate related data by partitioning the output files by the `region` column. Which PySpark write option achieves this?

options.text: `df.write.format('delta').bucketBy(8, 'region').save('/tables/sales')`
`df.write.format('delta').partitionBy('region').save('/tables/sales')`
`df.write.format('delta').sortBy('region').save('/tables/sales')`
`df.write.format('delta').option('partitionColumn', 'region').save('/tables/sales')`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. `partitionBy('region')` instructs Spark to create a separate directory for each distinct value of the `region` column (e.g., `region=US/`, `region=EU/`). Each partition's files are co-located in the same directory, enabling partition pruning for queries that filter on `region`.

**A** is incorrect because `bucketBy` creates hash buckets within a Hive-managed table context and is not applicable to Delta table writes with `.save()` — it does not create directory-level partitions.

**C** is incorrect because `sortBy` sorts data within existing partitions but does not create new partition directories based on column values.

**D** is incorrect because `option('partitionColumn', 'region')` is a JDBC read option used to parallelize reads from a relational database, not a Delta write option.

[What is Delta Lake in Databricks?](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/delta-intro.html)

questions.stem:
A data engineer runs the following command:

```python
(df.write
  .format('delta')
  .mode('overwrite')
  .option('partitionOverwriteMode', 'dynamic')
  .partitionBy('date')
  .save('/tables/events'))
```

Select TWO statements that correctly describe the behavior of dynamic partition overwrite mode. (Select TWO)

options.text: Only the date partitions present in the DataFrame `df` are overwritten; partitions not represented in `df` are preserved
All existing partitions in the target table are deleted and replaced with the partitions in `df`, regardless of which dates are in `df`
Dynamic partition overwrite is equivalent to an append — it adds new partitions but never removes old ones
This mode requires setting `spark.sql.sources.partitionOverwriteMode` to `dynamic` either in Spark config or via the `partitionOverwriteMode` option

questions.explanation:
**A** and **D** are correct. Dynamic partition overwrite mode changes the semantics of `mode('overwrite')` so that only the partitions represented in the incoming DataFrame are replaced — partitions not present in the DataFrame are preserved (**A**). This behavior is enabled either via the `partitionOverwriteMode` write option (as shown) or by setting `spark.sql.sources.partitionOverwriteMode=dynamic` in the Spark session config (**D**).

**B** is incorrect because deleting all existing partitions regardless of what is in the DataFrame describes the default static overwrite behavior — `partitionOverwriteMode=dynamic` was specifically introduced to avoid this.

**C** is incorrect because dynamic partition overwrite does replace the specific partitions present in the DataFrame — it is not a pure append operation; existing data in matching partitions is overwritten.

[What is Delta Lake in Databricks?](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/delta-intro.html)

questions.stem: A streaming pipeline processes events with a `trigger(processingTime='30 seconds')` configuration. What does this setting control?

options.text: The stream will only retain events that arrive within 30 seconds of the current time; older events are discarded
The stream will wait 30 seconds after each micro-batch completes before shutting down automatically
The stream will attempt to start a new micro-batch every 30 seconds, regardless of how much data has arrived
The stream writes output every 30 records, not every 30 seconds

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. `trigger(processingTime='30 seconds')` configures the stream to attempt to start a new micro-batch at most every 30 seconds. If a micro-batch completes in less than 30 seconds, Spark waits until the interval elapses before starting the next one. If a micro-batch takes longer, the next one starts immediately after.

**A** is incorrect because the processing time trigger has nothing to do with event timestamps or late data handling — discarding old events is the domain of watermarks, not trigger intervals.

**B** is incorrect because the trigger controls the interval between micro-batches during a running stream, not a shutdown timer — the stream continues indefinitely until explicitly stopped.

**D** is incorrect because `processingTime` is a time-based trigger, not a record-count-based trigger — it measures wall clock time, not the number of records processed.

[Configure Structured Streaming trigger intervals](https://docs.databricks.com/structured-streaming/triggers.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to use `outputMode('update')` for a streaming aggregation. Which condition must be true for update mode to produce correct results?

options.text: Update mode requires a watermark so Spark can clean up state for expired windows
Update mode can be combined with any stateful aggregation and does not require a watermark, but it is most suitable for sinks that support row-level updates (such as JDBC or Delta with MERGE)
Update mode requires the output sink to be a Kafka topic; Delta tables do not support update mode
Update mode is identical to complete mode — both rewrite the entire result set on every micro-batch

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. In `outputMode('update')`, Spark only emits rows whose aggregated values changed in each micro-batch (unlike complete mode which re-emits all rows). A watermark is not strictly required for update mode — the aggregation continues indefinitely — but a watermark is needed if you want Spark to eventually drop state for old windows to prevent unbounded state growth. Update mode is best paired with sinks that can handle updates, such as JDBC databases or Delta with `foreachBatch` and `MERGE INTO`.

**A** is incorrect because update mode does not strictly require a watermark — it works without one but will accumulate unbounded state for windowed aggregations without state cleanup.

**C** is incorrect because Delta tables are a valid sink for update mode via `foreachBatch` + `MERGE INTO` — Kafka is not a requirement.

**D** is incorrect because update mode emits only the rows that changed in the current micro-batch, while complete mode re-emits the entire aggregation result every batch.

[MERGE INTO](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/delta-merge-into.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to limit an Auto Loader stream so it processes at most 100 files per micro-batch, regardless of how many new files are available. Which option should they set?

options.text: `.option('cloudFiles.maxBytesPerTrigger', '100')`
`.option('trigger', 'once')`
`.option('cloudFiles.batchSize', '100')`
`.option('cloudFiles.maxFilesPerTrigger', '100')`

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. `cloudFiles.maxFilesPerTrigger` sets the maximum number of new files Auto Loader will include in a single micro-batch trigger. Setting it to `'100'` means each micro-batch processes at most 100 new files, even if hundreds more are available in the source directory.

**A** is incorrect because `cloudFiles.maxBytesPerTrigger` limits processing by total byte volume per trigger, not by file count — it controls size-based rate limiting, not file count.

**B** is incorrect because `trigger(once=True)` (or `trigger(availableNow=True)`) is a trigger mode that processes all available data in a single run then stops — it does not limit the number of files per micro-batch in a continuously running stream.

**C** is incorrect because `cloudFiles.batchSize` is not a valid Auto Loader option name — the correct option for per-trigger file limits is `cloudFiles.maxFilesPerTrigger`.

[What is Auto Loader?](https://docs.databricks.com/ingestion/auto-loader/index.html)

questions.stem:
A Databricks multi-task job uses task parameters to pass dynamic values between tasks. In Task A, the engineer sets a task value with:

```python
dbutils.jobs.taskValues.set(key='row_count', value=df.count())
```

How does Task B retrieve this value?

options.text: `spark.conf.get('taskValues.row_count')`
`dbutils.jobs.taskValues.get(taskKey='task_a', key='row_count')`
`dbutils.widgets.get('row_count')`
`os.environ['TASK_VALUE_row_count']`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. `dbutils.jobs.taskValues.get(taskKey, key)` retrieves a value that was set by another task. The `taskKey` parameter specifies which upstream task set the value (using the task key configured in the job definition), and `key` is the string key used when calling `set`. This is the purpose-built API for inter-task value passing in multi-task jobs.

**A** is incorrect because task values are not exposed as Spark configuration properties — `spark.conf.get` reads cluster and session configuration, not cross-task values.

**C** is incorrect because `dbutils.widgets` handles notebook widget parameters that are passed at job invocation time from the outside, not values passed between individual tasks within a job run.

**D** is incorrect because task values are not exposed as environment variables — they are stored in the job run context and accessed only via the `dbutils.jobs.taskValues` API.

[Lakeflow Jobs](https://docs.databricks.com/jobs/index.html)

questions.stem: A Databricks Job run fails partway through due to a transient infrastructure error. The engineer wants to retry only the failed and skipped tasks without re-running tasks that already succeeded. Which feature should they use?

options.text: Re-run the entire job from the beginning to ensure data consistency
Use the 'Repair Run' feature, which re-runs only the failed and downstream skipped tasks from the failed run while keeping the results of previously succeeded tasks
Create a new job run with only the failed task configured as the sole task
Manually re-execute the failed notebook in a separate all-purpose cluster and update the job run status

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. The 'Repair Run' feature in Databricks Jobs allows engineers to retry a failed job run by re-executing only the tasks that failed or were skipped (due to upstream failures), while preserving the output of tasks that already succeeded. This avoids unnecessary recomputation and is the standard way to handle transient failures in multi-task jobs.

**A** is incorrect because re-running the entire job from the beginning wastes time and compute by re-executing already-successful tasks, and may produce inconsistent results if upstream tasks produce non-idempotent side effects.

**C** is incorrect because creating a new job with only the failed task is a fragile workaround that bypasses proper orchestration, loses the original job run context, and breaks dependency management.

**D** is incorrect because manually running a notebook in a separate cluster and patching the job status is an anti-pattern that undermines job tracking, lineage, and auditability.

[Troubleshoot and repair job failures](https://docs.databricks.com/jobs/repair-job-failures.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer granted SELECT on a table to a user last month. The user no longer needs access. Which SQL command correctly removes the SELECT privilege from that user?

options.text: `DELETE GRANT SELECT ON TABLE prod.sales.orders FROM user@company.com`
`REVOKE SELECT ON TABLE prod.sales.orders FROM user@company.com`
`DROP PRIVILEGE SELECT ON TABLE prod.sales.orders FOR user@company.com`
`DENY SELECT ON TABLE prod.sales.orders TO user@company.com`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. `REVOKE <privilege> ON <object> FROM <principal>` is the standard SQL command for removing a previously granted privilege in Unity Catalog. This mirrors the standard SQL privilege management pattern of `GRANT` to add and `REVOKE` to remove.

**A** is incorrect because `DELETE GRANT` is not valid SQL syntax in Unity Catalog or standard SQL — the correct verb is `REVOKE`.

**C** is incorrect because `DROP PRIVILEGE` is not valid SQL syntax in any SQL dialect supported by Databricks.

**D** is incorrect because `DENY` does not exist as a privilege command in Unity Catalog. This is a common misconception from other systems (like SQL Server) where `DENY` explicitly blocks access. In Unity Catalog, absence of a `GRANT` means no access — there is no `DENY` concept.

[REVOKE privilege syntax](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/sql/language-manual/security-revoke)

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates the following view in Unity Catalog:

```sql
CREATE VIEW prod.analytics.my_data
AS SELECT * FROM prod.raw.events
   WHERE owner_email = current_user();
```

What is the purpose of using `current_user()` in this view definition?

options.text: `current_user()` returns the name of the user who created the view, hard-coding their email into the view at creation time

`current_user()` is evaluated at query time and returns the email address of the user currently executing the query, implementing per-user row-level filtering without requiring separate views per user
`current_user()` returns the service principal associated with the cluster, not the interactive user
`current_user()` is a deprecated function and should be replaced with `session_user()`

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. `current_user()` is a Spark SQL/Unity Catalog built-in function that returns the identity of the user currently executing the query at runtime. When embedded in a view's WHERE clause like this, it enables dynamic row-level security: each user who queries `my_data` sees only rows where `owner_email` matches their own identity, without needing separate views per user.

**A** is incorrect because `current_user()` is evaluated at query time, not at view creation time — it does not capture and hard-code the creator's identity permanently into the view definition.

**C** is incorrect because in Unity Catalog with user-level authentication (including personal access token auth), `current_user()` reflects the authenticated end user, not a generic service principal.

**D** is incorrect because `current_user()` is an actively supported and commonly used function in Databricks SQL and Spark SQL for implementing dynamic row-level filtering.

[What is Unity Catalog?](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/index.html)
